dailyJudgementSnippet: Google Gemini had the best mix of tone and coverage: Gemma 4 generated real offline iPhone experimentation despite caveats about battery use and production readiness [E5], while Gemini Robotics-ER drew mostly upbeat discussion about spatial reasoning, planning, and robotics use cases [E6].
winnerExplanation: Google Gemini won because it picked up two meaningful front-page signals and neither landed badly. The Gemma 4 iPhone thread was mixed but engaged, with commenters actually building on it and discussing practical constraints rather than dismissing it outright [E5]. The Gemini Robotics-ER thread was more clearly favorable, with curiosity about concrete robotics applications and praise for the broader planning/spatial-reasoning direction [E6]. That gave Google Gemini the strongest positive day once both sentiment and breadth were considered.

openai.judgementSnippet: ChatGPT for Excel drew interest and favorable comparison to incumbent spreadsheet assistants, but comments also focused on slow performance and workbook-sharing/privacy worries; separately, a security thread included a direct attack on OpenAI's security claims after a ChatGPT bypass anecdote [E1][E7].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Claude got a strong anecdotal endorsement for presentation work in the Excel thread, but the Claude privilege case kept attention on Terms of Service and confidentiality implications more than on model capability [E3][E4].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Gemma 4 prompted hands-on offline iPhone tinkering even as commenters called the current path a battery-hungry tech demo, and Gemini Robotics-ER benefited from curious, mostly upbeat discussion about spatial reasoning and robotics planning [E5][E6].

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: In the ChatGPT for Excel discussion, Copilot was mainly the negative reference point: commenters said Microsoft's Copilot buttons often just open a chat panel, and one explicitly called Copilot for Excel useless [E2].

evidence.summary: The ChatGPT for Excel launch brought meaningful OpenAI discussion, but comments were mixed: some were eager to try it and contrasted it favorably with Excel Copilot, while others complained about slow spreadsheet generation and warned that workbook contents may be shared with OpenAI.

In the same ChatGPT for Excel thread, Microsoft Copilot took direct criticism. A top comment said Copilot buttons across Microsoft products often just open a side chat, and 'Copilot for Excel is useless' because it cannot answer simple cell questions.
A top comment in the ChatGPT for Excel thread praised Claude Cowork for PowerPoint, saying its content and design quality were stunning and presenting it as a real threat to Microsoft's office tools.

The AI-privilege story centered on Claude chats. Comments treated it as a close legal question, but repeatedly raised concerns about how Claude's Terms of Service and third-party handling might affect confidentiality and privilege.

The Gemma 4 on iPhone story showed genuine maker interest, including a commenter who built an offline 'pocket vibe coder' with Gemma 4, but other comments said the current implementation is GPU-routed, battery-hungry, and more of a flashy tech demo than a production tool.

The Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 thread was mostly upbeat and future-facing. Commenters highlighted gauge-reading and rover-driving examples, discussed Gemini's planning and spatial-reasoning strengths, and explored concrete robotics applications.

In the cybersecurity thread, one top comment described using an AI product plus browser tooling to bypass ChatGPT protections and concluded that, given OpenAI's security boasts, those defenses were 'worthless,' creating a direct negative signal on OpenAI's security credibility.
